%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand how matrices are added, subtracted, multiplied, and scaled — and recognize the size conditions that make each operation legal.

Section B.6-2 introduces the arithmetic of matrices. Just as numbers can be added and multiplied, matrices follow their own rules — but with one critical twist: **size matters**. Two matrices can only be added if they have the same dimensions, and they can only be multiplied if the inner dimensions match. After this section you will be able to perform matrix addition, scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication correctly, verify properties like \(AI = IA = A\), and avoid the most common exam trap: assuming matrix multiplication is commutative when it is not.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Three core matrix operations: addition (same size required), scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication (row dot column). The red callout flags the most common exam trap.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777213898777-8494.png)

## Core Idea

The central relationship in matrix algebra is the **row-times-column rule** for multiplication, and the **size compatibility condition** that governs every operation.

**Addition** is element-wise: if \(A\) and \(B\) are both \(m \times n\), then \((A+B)_{ij} = a_{ij} + b_{ij}\). Mismatched sizes make addition undefined — full stop.

**Matrix multiplication** is more subtle. The entry \(c_{ij}\) of the product \(C = AB\) is computed as:

$$c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{ik} b_{kj}$$

This means row \(i\) of \(A\) is dotted with column \(j\) of \(B\). For this to work, \(A\) must be \(m \times n\) and \(B\) must be \(n \times p\) — the inner dimensions must match.

### EXAM TRAP

Matrix multiplication is **not commutative**: \(AB \neq BA\) in general. The identity matrix is the one exception: \(AI = IA = A\).

**Minimal example:** If \(A\) is \(2 \times 3\) and \(B\) is \(3 \times 4\), then \(AB\) is \(2 \times 4\), but \(BA\) is undefined.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Matrix addition requires identical dimensions; add element by element.
- Matrix multiplication uses the row-dot-column rule; inner dimensions must match.
- \(AB \neq BA\) in general — commutativity does NOT hold for matrix products.

*In the next section we will examine the transpose and inverse of a matrix, and see how these operations interact with the multiplication rules established here.*
